provenance.source_corpus: Peat, Water and Plant Tissue Chemistry Monitoring: A Seven-Year Case-Study in a Restored Peatland

source_spans.text: Following ecosystem-scale restoration of a post-harvested bog in Eastern Canada, a long term monitoring program was set up.
The following spring phosphorus fertilization was applied where granular phosphate rock (0-13-0; McInnes Natural Fertilizers) was spread at the rate of 15 g m-2.
Among other, it highlighted P deficiencies in the non-restored section, and nutrient replenishment in the restored plant tissues.
P levels appear to be a good indicator of restoration success and should be used routinely in monitoring studies.
an average of 430 g m-2 of Sphagnum biomass had already accumulated after seven years and keeps accumulating
Na was more concentrated in the restored peat
Fe (total and soluble) and N (NH4 and Ntot) were generally more concentrated in the non-restored peat
Fe (total and soluble) and N (NH4 and Ntot) were generally more concentrated in the non-restored peat
P fertilization probably enhanced the capacity of Sphagnum to assimilate N
